coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis of gold nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots-phthalocyanine nanoplatforms: First description of the photophysical and surface enhanced Raman scattering behaviour
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Nwahara, Nnamdi
Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the need for new low-dimensional molecular assemblies with tailored electronic properties, the current study presents a facile approach for the synthesis and assembly of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) onto functional graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-phthalocyanines (Pcs) arrays and the investigation of their photophysical and surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) properties. The GQDs were functionalized with L-glutathione (GSH) (to form GQDs@GSH) in order to assist coupling to the low symmetry Zn tris–(tert–butyl) mono carboxyphenoxy (propionic acid) phthalocyanine (complex 1) to form 1@GQDs. The affinity of gold (Au) to sulphur (S) was exploited for the assembly of the AuNPs onto 1@GQDs platform to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs. Transmission electron microscopic investigations confirmed the formation of monodispersed, spherical Pc/GQDs@GSH/AuNPs hybrids. The nanocomposite displayed high triplet quantum yields, which translated into high singlet oxygen quantum yield as high as 87%. Furthermore, the formed composites demonstrated strong surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) properties with an unprecedented intrinsic maximal enhancement factor of more than 30-fold. These nanostructures also retain more than 90% of their original SERS intensities after a week of storage, displaying superb stability under ambient conditions. These results highlight the remarkable potential of this composite as a unique Raman-based PDT dosimetric agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metallophthalocyanines
Graphene quantum dots
Photophysics
Surface enhanced raman scattering
Gold nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Zn tris–(tert–butyl) mono carboxyphenoxy (propionic acid) phthalocyanine (1) (Scheme 1)
Synthesis of AuNPs on 1@GQDs to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs (Scheme 2)
Synthesis and characterization of complex 1 and its conjugates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the preparation of 1@GQDs-AuNPs with illustration of covalent interaction of GQDs@GSH and complex 1, and the assembly of AuNPs onto the Pcs/GQDs@GSH surface.
FTIR spectra of complex 1 alone (a), GQDs@GSH (b), 1@GQDs (c) and 1@GQDs-AuNPs (d) showing successful formation all composites.
Ground state absorption spectra of GQDs@GSH alone, complex 1, 1@GQDs, and 1@GQDs-AuNPs in DMSO.
Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of 1@GQDs-AuNPs in DMSO.
TEM images of GQDs@GSH, 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs showing formation of monodispersed 1@GQDs-AuNPs.
AFM images of (A) 1@GQDs and (B) 1@GQDs-AuNPs with corresponding 2D height profiles below (a and b), respectively.
Representative EDX spectra of complex 1, 1@GQDs, 1@GQDs-AuNPs showing all expected elements.
DLS graphs showing average particle sizes for the GQDs@GSH alone, 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs highlighting resultant changes in sizes upon modification of GQDs@GSH with complex 1 and then AuNPs.
XRD patterns for (a) complex 1 alone, (b) GQDs@GSH, (c) 1@GQDs, and (d) 1@GQDs-AuNPs.

Raman spectra of GQDs@GSH (i), 1@GQDs (ii), and 1@GQDs-AuNPs (iii) showing the corresponding Raman intensity changes of the GQDs@GSH upon conjugation to complex 1, and chemisorption of AuNPs to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs.

Raman spectra of 1 mg/ml each of GQDs@GSH alone, 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs composites showwing that the AuNPs were formed on the sulphur containing Pcs/GQDs@GSH nanoplatform. This resulted in the observed enhancement of the Raman properties of the Pc/GQDs complexes, afforded by the successful chemisorption of the AuNPs.

Raman spectrum of 1@GQDs-AuNPs showing the high reproducibility of Raman signals of 1@GQDs-AuNPs with RSD of 1.01 %. 14 Raman measurements were taken after 7 days of storage without any marked differences in the Raman positions or peak intensities.
Typical fluorescence decay curve of 1@GQDs-AuNPs in DMSO at excitation wavelength of 670 nm.

(A) Representative excited state triplet absorption decay curves for 1 alone, 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs in DMSO at excitation wavelength of 674 nm. (B) Triplet decay for various concentrations at 490 nm (a) 2 × 10−5 M (second order); (b) 2 × 10−6 M (second order); (c) 2 × 10−7 M (first order) for complex 1.
Sample spectra showing the generation of singlet oxygen using DPBF as a single oxygen quencher for 1@GQDs - AuNPs in DMSO.
Photophysicochemical data of complex 1 and conjugates in DMSO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sizes in brackets is for GQDs alone.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Absorption bands for GQDS alone are in brackets.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are when GQDs when linked.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Value in brackets is the SPR band of AuNPs in the conjugate.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are for excitation where GQDs absorb. Fluorescence quantum yield value for GQDs@GSH is 0.27 and lifetime is 3.4 ns.

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis of gold nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots-phthalocyanine nanoplatforms: First description of the photophysical and surface enhanced Raman scattering behaviour

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, P.O. 94, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold nanoparticles were assembled onto graphene quantum dots –phthalocyanines arrays with improved singlet oxygen production and strong surface enhanced Raman scattering properties.

Owing to the need for new low-dimensional molecular assemblies with tailored electronic properties, the current study presents a facile approach for the synthesis and assembly of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) onto functional graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-phthalocyanines (Pcs) arrays and the investigation of their photophysical and surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) properties. The GQDs were functionalized with L-glutathione (GSH) (to form GQDs@GSH) in order to assist coupling to the low symmetry Zn tris–(tert–butyl) mono carboxyphenoxy (propionic acid) phthalocyanine (complex 1) to form 1@GQDs. The affinity of gold (Au) to sulphur (S) was exploited for the assembly of the AuNPs onto 1@GQDs platform to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs. Transmission electron microscopic investigations confirmed the formation of monodispersed, spherical Pc/GQDs@GSH/AuNPs hybrids. The nanocomposite displayed high triplet quantum yields, which translated into high singlet oxygen quantum yield as high as 87%. Furthermore, the formed composites demonstrated strong surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) properties with an unprecedented intrinsic maximal enhancement factor of more than 30-fold. These nanostructures also retain more than 90% of their original SERS intensities after a week of storage, displaying superb stability under ambient conditions. These results highlight the remarkable potential of this composite as a unique Raman-based PDT dosimetric agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a treatment modality which employs the combined action of a photosensitizer (PS) and specific light sources for the treatment of various cancers [1,2]. With the advantages of being non-invasive with site-specific activation and low systemic damage, PDT has emerged as an attractive alternative to surgery or radiation [3–5]. Metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) have been found to be viable photosensitizers (PS) for PDT [6–8]. Properties such as a distinctive intense absorption in the red region of the visible spectrum as well as high singlet oxygen generation ability [6,8] make MPcs suitable candidates for PDT.

On the other hand, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have gained interest owing to their low cytotoxicity and ability to act as photothermal therapy (PTT) agents [9–11]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are also known PDT agents [12,13]. Combining AuNPs or GQDs with MPcs is known to result in improved triplet and singlet oxygen quantum yields [14,15]. Singlet oxygen is the cytotoxic species for PDT. However, MPcs-nanoparticles conjugates often suffer from very low fluorescence [15,16]; an important parameter in PDT dosimetry [17]. Various matrices such as the amount of PS at the treatment site before and after PDT are monitored by fluorescence and are hence utilized for PDT dose design [18,19]. Consequently, the interplay between fluorescence and intersystem crossing from the excited singlet state must be carefully negotiated in the design of suitable PS for PDT, which is often a challenge [20,21], necessitating the development of alternative techniques to PDT dosimetry. The current work is aimed at creating a novel nanoconjugate that can do both imaging and PDT. PDT activity of the phthalocyanine will improve due to increased singlet oxygen in the presence of AuNPs as a result of the heavy atom effect of the latter. Increase in singlet oxygen quantum yields, means there is increased intersystem crossing to the triplet state from which singlet oxygen is produced. This inevitably lowers fluorescence, hence lowering imaging ability of the phthalocyanine. To counteract the problem of reduced fluorescence in phthalocyanine, the imaging will occur through the Raman spectra of GQDs in the presence of AuNPs in the nanocomposite. Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity of GQDs is expected to increase in the presence of AuNPs. Colloidal metal nanostructures are known to give rise to local electromagnetic fields normally referred to as electromagnetic ‘hot-spots’, which result in significantly enhanced Raman signals [22]. Hot spots in graphitic structures such as GQDs may also result due to the presence of CO bonds, which provide active sites for the adsorption of metal atoms or clusters [23]. AuNPs on their own are SERS agents [24]. Unfortunately, metallic nanoparticles based SERS agents commonly suffer irreversible aggregation of the nanoparticles as well as low reproducibility [25,26]. Hence AuNPs are used in this work to improve the SERS activity of GQDs.

SERS has gained attractiveness over fluorescence-based bioimaging techniques as a non-destructive photon-scattering technique, which provides specific molecular fingerprints, enabling identification [27]. This is due to the high signal-to-noise ratio that can be achieved in complex biological systems even at single wavelength excitation in the near infrared (NIR) region. Unlike fluorescence imaging techniques, Raman scattering is resistant to quenching and photobleaching [27], and is suitable for long-term tracking and labelling [28,29]. These critical features have encouraged the recent interest in Raman-based PDT dosimetry [30–33]. GQDs exhibit distinctive Raman scattering features with sharp and strong Raman peaks that can be easily distinguished from fluorescence backgrounds, and are thus suitable for Raman imaging [34].

In the present study, GQDs are functionalized with glutathione (GSH) to form GQDs@GSH in order to allow for linking of the NH2 group of GSH with COOH group on the ZnPc derivative (complex 1) to form 1@GQDs. It is possible that ππ interaction between complex 1 and GQDs also occurs. AuNPs are then grown on the 1@GQDs hybrid to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs. The phthalocyanine employed in this work is the Zn tris–(tert–butyl) mono carboxyphenoxy (propionic acid) phthalocyanine (1). The choice of propanoic acid as a substituent is based on the fact that porphyrins containing propanoic acid have been employed successfully for PDT [8]. We have reported on ZnPc derivatives mono and tetrasubstituted with propionic acid [35], with no other substituents. In this work the ZnPc contains one propionic ligand and three tert-butyl substituents, the latter improves solubility [36]. We employed a low symmetry ZnPc since asymmetry is known to improves the photophysical properties of porphyrin-type complexes [37]. In addition, the COOH group will allow for linking to the L-glutathione (GSH) functionalised GQDs@GSH via an amide bond. GQDs were capped with GSH by adsorption of the latter on the former, forming GQDs@GSH as described before [14]. The presence of GSH on the GQDs@GSH also facilitates the linking of AuNPs through the Au-S bond [38]. First complex 1 was linked to GQDs@GSH via an amide bond to form 1@GQDs. Then AuNPs were prepared in situ onto 1@GQDs to form 1@GQDs-AuNps.

To the best our knowledge, such dual functional nanoplatforms, comprising Pcs, GQDs@GSH and AuNPs are not known and are thus reported for the first time. This work demonstrated for the first time, the possibility of both efficient singlet oxygen generation and significant Raman scattering potential in a nanosystem, demonstrating its potential as a novel PDT dosimetric agent.

The following compounds were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich: trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), zinc (II) acetate dihydrate, 4-tert-butylphthalonitrile (a), quinine sulfate, and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium borohydride and N,N dimethylformade (DMF) were obtained from SAARCHEM. N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc), and tetrahydrofuran (THF) were purchased from Merck. Dimethylsulphoxide (DMSO) and 1,8-diazabicyclo[5,4,0]undec-7-ene (DBU) were obtained from Fluka. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water obtained from a Milli-Q Water system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA, USA). All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and were of analytical grade and used as received. The syntheses of 3((4-phenoxy)-propanoic acid) phthalonitrile (b) [35] and GQDs@GSH [14] were as reported in literature. Equipment items employed in this work are provided in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zn tris–(tert–butyl) mono carboxyphenoxy (propionic acid) phthalocyanine (1) (Scheme 1)
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 3((4-phenoxy)-propanoic acid) phthalonitrile (b) (0.20 g, 0.68 mmol), 4-tert-butyl phthalonitrile (a) (0.460 g, 0.0025 mol), zinc (II) acetate dihydrate (0.25 g, 1.1 mmol) and DBU (0.3 mL), dissolved in dry 1-pentanol (3 mL) was refluxed at 180 °C with constant stirring, in the presence of nitrogen for 6 h. The product was precipitated out of solution with methanol, followed by purification using silica packed column and a solvent mixture of methanol:THF (1:9) as eluent to give a blue powder. 40% yield (w/w); UV–vis (DMSO) λmax/nm (Log Ɛ): 678 (5.42), 611 (4.67), 352 (4.96). MALDI-TOF MS (m/z): calcd 910.39: found 909.86 IR (ATR): ν (cm−1): 3272 (OH), 3053 (Ar-CH), 2953 (CH), 1719 (CO), 1478 (ArCN). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO) δ 9.14 (s, 2H, Ar-H), 7.84 (d, J = 39.2 Hz, 5H, Ar-H), 7.41–7.27 (m, 5H, Ar-H), 7.09 (s, 2H), 6.68 (s, 2H, Ar-H), 3.18 (s, 4H, −CH2), 2.10 (s, 27H, −CH3) .

1@GQDs was synthesized as follows: a mixture of complex 1, (20 mg, 0.26 mmol), DCC (0.025 g, 0.12 mmol), and NHS (0.013 g, 0.11 mmol) in DMSO (3 mL) was gently stirred at room temperature for 48 h to activate the carboxylic groups of 1. GQDs@GSH (10 mg) was then added and the reaction mixture stirred for a further 48 h at ambient temperature. The resultant conjugate (1@GQDs) was precipitated out from solution with trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and ice. The precipitate was washed several times with water, centrifuged, and then dried in the fume hood to obtain the 1@GQDs (Scheme 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuNPs on 1@GQDs to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs (Scheme 2)
ce:section.ce:para: The in situ synthesis of the AuNPs on 1@GQDs nanoplatform (to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs) was as follows: to a solution of 1@GQDs (20 mg) in 3 mL DMF, NaBH4 (1 mg, 0.053 mol) was added. To this solution tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O, 0.12 mmol) was quickly added and the resulting solution stirred vigorously for 2 min. Upon completion, the solution changed from green to light purple and the resulting conjugate began to precipitate out of solution. The precipitate was then repeatedly washed with water, centrifuged and dried in a fume hood to obtain 1@GQDs-AuNPs conjugates (Scheme 2).

The 1064 nm laser was employed and operated at a power of 150 mW. An integration time of 100 ms was employed in the Raman spectral measurements. To appreciate the SERS effect, the enhancement factor (EF) of 1@GQDs-AuNPs when compared to GQDs@GSH alone or 1@GQDs was quantitatively determined according to Eq. (1) [39].
(1)
E F =
I
S E R S
C
R a m a n
I
R a m a n
C
S E R S
where ISERS are the Raman spectral intensities of 1@GQDs in the presence or absence of AuNPs, while IRaman represents spectral intensities of GQDs@GSH alone. CRaman is the approximated concentration of the GQDs@GSH alone and CSERS represents approximated concentrations of GQDs in 1@GQDs or 1@GQDs-AuNPs. Equations (S1–S3) for the estimation of the concentration of GQDs are shown in the Supporting information. For our experiments, the D-band as chosen as our vibrational mode for calculations of the enhancement factor. The background signal was subtracted from the recorded Raman spectra and the resulting data were individually normalized with respect to the integrated area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of complex 1 and its conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the synthetic pathway for complex 1, which involves a cyclotetramerization of the corresponding phthalonitriles in the presence of catalytic DBU, Zn metal salt, and 1-pentanol. 1H NMR spectrum for complex 1 shown in Fig. S1 A (Supporting information). The aromatic and aliphatic protons were observed between 9.14–6.68 pm and 3.18–2.10 ppm, respectively [Fig. S1 (Supporting information)]. Peak integration gave the anticipated total number of protons, confirming the relative purity of the complex. Mass spectral data was in agreement with the proposed structure for complex 1 (Fig. S2 (Supporting information). It has been shown that there is a high probability of multiple heavy isotopes with an increase in the mass of a molecule [40]. The existence of multiple isotopes leads to a decrease in the relative abundance of the monoisotopic peak, hence the isotopic distribution model has been shown to be vital for accurate assignment of molecular mass in large molecules [40], such as phthalocyanines. Fig. S2 shows the simulated masses of complex 1 using isotopic distribution model as well as the experimentally observed masses. It can be seen that all the complexes showed fragmentation with molecular ion peak as [M+1]+.

The well-known affinity of Au to S was exploited for the in-situ synthesis of 1@GQDs-AuNPs. The synthesized AuNPs are assembled onto the GQDs@GSH surface by Au–S bond due to the sulphur groups (owing to the presence of GSH) of the as-synthesized GQDs@GSH. The FTIR spectrum of the in-situ synthesized 1@GQDs-AuNPs is shown in Fig. 1(d). The CH bands of 1@GQDs at 2932 cm−1 were blue-shifted to 2923 cm−1 in 1@GQDs-AuNPs. This may be ascribed to the interaction affinity of S-H groups to Au surface, which has been observed to change the vibration of CH bonds [41]. In addition, The SH band (which is clearly visible in GQDs@GSH at 2505 cm−1) is absent in 1@GQDs-AuNPs. This also suggests the breaking of SH bond of the thiol in order to form the S-Au bond [42].

UV/VIS absorption spectroscopy was used to study the electronic absorption behaviour of the complexes in DMSO. The ground state absorption spectra of complex 1 showed two bands at 361 and 678 nm in DMSO, Table 1 , Fig. 2 , which can be attributed to the characteristic Soret and Q bands, respectively. The narrow Q band of complex 1 is typical of metallated phthalocyanines and obeyed Beer’s law at concentrations below 8.0 × 10−5 M. The Q band maxima for complex 1 in 1@GQDs remained unchanged (Table 1, Fig. 2). There was however an enhancement in the B-band region of the spectrum complex 1@GQDs, attributed to the presence of GQDs@GSH. The absorption spectra of GQDs have been associated with the n-π* transition of the sp2 carbon electrons of the graphene core of GQDs@GSH [43]. The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) bands of AuNPs in the 1@GQDs-AuNPS was observed at 525 nm confirming the successful in situ formation of AuNPs on the surface of the GQDs@GSH/Pcs nanoplatform (Fig. 2).

Since the approximate size of Pcs (1 nm) is far less than that of the GQDs@GSH, it is possible that more than one Pc is linked to the GQDs@GSH. The loading of complex 1 onto the surfaces and edges of GQDs@GSH was determined following literature reports [44]. The loading was about the same for the conjugates, Table 1.

The normalized absorption, emission, and excitation spectra of the complex 1 and 1@GQDs-AuNPs are shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting information) and Fig. 3 , respectively. For all complexes, the excitation spectrum was found to be a mirror image of the emission spectrum, and the later was the same as the absorption spectra (except for the presence of the SPR band in the absorption spectra of 1@GQDs-AuNPs) with slight changes being due to different types of equipment used. The closeness of the absorption and excitation maxima, shows that the absorbing species as the same as the emitting one and suggests that they do not readily aggregate in solution.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to assess the morphologies of the GQDs@GSH alone and in the presence of complex 1 and AuNPs, Fig. 4 . GQDs@GSH were monodispersed with an overall quasi-spherical morphology and particle size average of about 5 nm. Monodispersity was also observed in the 1@GQDs conjugates. The size of the GQDs@GSH was found to increase however, following conjugation to complex 1. The TEM image shown in Fig. 4 clearly shows monodispersed composites of 1@GQDs-AuNPs, highlighting minimal aggregation in the composites, unlike many conventional SERS hybrids [26]. There were also observed size increases of the 1@GQDs upon decoration with AuNPs. The increase in size following conjugation confirms the successful modification at each stage and could be as attributed to π–π interaction of MPcs on adjacent NPs [45,46], which is possible in phthalocyanines.

The AFM images of as-synthesized GQDs@GSH show the topographic morphology of the GQDs@GSH (Fig. S4(A) and (B)). As illustrated in Fig. S4, the average height of GQDs@GSH is about 1.2 nm. As single-layered graphene quantum dots or graphene oxide sheets have the thickness of 0.34 nm by AFM measurements [47], we estimated that the synthesized GQDs@GSH should contain about three-four graphene layers [48,49]. The calculated surface roughness of 0.52 nm of the synthesized GQDs@GSH demonstrates their homogeneity. The topographic morphology of 1@GQDs shows an increase in topographical heights to about 6 nm (Fig. 5 (A)) and surface roughness value of 2.48 nm. The increase in topographical height could be evidence of the successful conjugation of the GQDs to complex 1. Meanwhile, marked changes in surface morphology were noted for 1@GQDs-AuNPs compared to that of either GQDs alone or 1@GQDs (Fig. 5(B)). 1@GQDs-AuNPs showed an uneven surface with wrinkles and voids and root mean square (RMS) roughness of 18.3 nm. The roughness introduced by the presence of the AuNPs is beneficial for SERS as rough metal surfaces give rise to local electromagnetic fields, which may give rise to significantly enhanced Raman signals [50,51].

The elemental composition of complex 1, and its respective conjugates, 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs were deduced qualitatively using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Fig. 6 ). The results obtained were consistent with the expected elemental composition for complex 1, which confirmed successful synthesis. Additional S peaks attributed to the presence of GQDs@GSH was observed in 1@GQDs, and well as Au peaks owing to the successful assembly of AuNPs on the Pcs/GQDs@GSH nanoplatform to form 1@GQDs-AuNPs (Fig. 6).

The average particles sizes determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) were 5.6 nm, 15.7 nm and 28.2 nm for GQDs@GSH alone, 1@GQDs, and 1@GQDs-AuNPs, respectively (Fig. 7 , Table 1). DLS results show that there was increase in the sizes of the GQDs@GSH nanoconjugates compared to GQDs@GSH alone showing successful formation of 1@GQDs and the 1@GQDs-AuNPs.

The differently conjugated systems were studied by X-Ray diffraction (XRD) to show the various effects on the Braggs reflections. The broad XRD pattern for complex 1 (Fig. 8 (a)) is typical of phthalocyanines [52], and suggests an amorphous form. GQDs@GSH exhibit a broad XRD diffraction peak at 2θ = 250, which is due to (002) Bragg’s reflection typical of lattice spacing of graphene based nanomaterials [53], and is reflective of their small size (Fig. 8(b)). Fig. 8(c) shows the XRD of 1@GQDs with a broad peak at 2θ = 250, which is indicative of an amorphous conjugate. There are however small sharp peaks present in the conjugate (Fig. 8(c)). Such peaks embedded in a broad XRD pattern have been suggested to reflect increase in size subsequent to conjugation [54]. Upon assembly of the AuNPs onto the 1@GQDs, peaks attributable to AuNPs were observed (Fig. 8(d)), confirming successful assembly of the AuNPs onto the 1@GQDs. The AuNPs diffraction pattern displayed distinct Braggs reflections corresponding to planes {111}, {200}, {220}, and {311} at 2θ = 38.33, 44.38, 64.53, and 78.18°. The AuNPs peaks, indexed to the face centred cubic crystal structure of the gold nanoparticles conclusively proves the formation of gold nanocrystals with reference to the crystal structures from the PCPDFWIN: Au (#04-0784) [54].

The extent of defects or quality of the graphene core can be determined by measuring the ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG). For the GQDs@GSH alone the calculated ID/IG was 0.16. The ID/IG increased to 0.20 upon conjugation of GQDs@GSH to complex 1. This increase in the D-band upon modification complex 1 confirms increases in the structural disorder of the GQDs@GSH [57]. There was further increases in ID/IG upon self-assembly of the in-situ synthesized AuNPs to 4.25 due to Au-S bonds formed.

In a typical SERS study process, 1 mg/ml each of either GQDs@GSH alone or 1@GQDs or 1@GQDs-AuNPs were added into a quartz cell and the respective Raman spectra obtained. As expected, highly enhanced Raman peaks with good signal-to-noise ratio were observed for 1@GQDs-AuNPs as opposed to GQDs@GSH or 1@GQDs alone (Fig. 10), with vibrational frequencies comparable to those of GQDs@GSH [57,62]. The SERS enhancement factor (EF) is an important parameter for the performance demonstration of SERS substrates. The D-band was chosen as the representation peak for EF calculation using Eq. (1). An overall 32-fold enhancement was observed based on D-band analysis (Fig. 10) when using 1@GQDs-AuNPs. These results show the unique Raman features of such supramolecular hybrids. The observed Raman enhancement can be explained to have resulted from the AuNPs being chemisorbed onto the GQDs@GSH/Pcs hybrid. By measuring the SERS signal of 1@GQDs-AuNPs 7 days after preparation using the D-band, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be about 1.01% evidencing the excellent SERS signal uniformity and reproducibility of Raman signal obtained (Fig. 11 ).

The fluorescence (ΦF quantum yields were assessed using comparative methods reported in literature [63,64], using unsubstituted ZnPc in DMSO where MPc absorbs with the following reference values: ΦF = 0.20) [64] and quinine sulfate in H2SO4 (0.05 M) (ΦF = 0.52) [65] when exciting where GQDs absorb. The fluorescence quantum yields of the GQDs in the conjugates
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)
C o n j u g a t e
were calculated using Eq. (2).
(2)
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)
C o n j u g a t e
=
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)
F
F
(
G Q D s
)
c o n j u g a t e
F
(
G Q D s
)
where
F
F
(
G Q D s
)
c o n j u g a t e
and
F
(
G Q D s
)
are the fluorescence intensities of QDs in Pc-GQDs conjugates and GQDs@GSH alone, respectively. ΦF(GQDS) represents fluorescence quantum yield of the QDs alone and was used as a standard.

The fluorescence life-time (τF) values for complex 1 and the resultant composites were determined using a time correlated single photon count (TCSPC) method, following excitation at the emission maxima. Fig. 12 shows a typical fluorescence decay curve for the 1@GQDs at 670 nm excitation. For all composites, a mono exponential decay resulted. When exciting where complex 1 absorbs, there is a decrease in the fluorescence quantum yields and lifetimes for the 1@GQDs compared to 1 alone (Table 1). GQDs@GSH are fluorescent, and their fluorescence was also quenched in the presence of both complex 1 and AuNPs when exciting where the GQDs@GSH absorb (Table 1). The quenching of the GQDs@GSH emission by Pcs has been reported [53], and may be due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and other process that deactivate the excited state. A further decrease in the fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) and lifetime (τF) of 1@GQDs upon modification with AuNPs (and exciting where Pc or GQDs absorb) was observed (Table 1). This decrease is fluorescence in the presence of AuNPs is attributed to the heavy atom effects of metallic nanoparticles which encourage intersystem crossing to the triplet states of the phthalocyanine. Moreover, the close proximity of metallic nanoparticles to phthalocyanines has been reported to decrease the fluorescence quantum yields and fluorescence lifetime of the latter [66]. Thus, the observed decrease in florescence quantum yields and lifetimes could suggest close packing of the Pc on the surface of the AuNPs.

An important parameter in the evaluation of the as-synthesized Pc complexes is the triplet quantum yield (ФT). ФT represents the fraction of absorbing molecules that undergo intersystem crossing to the metastable triplet excited state. Higher triplet quantum yield can only be possible if the intersystem crossing lifetime is less than the triplet lifetime, which results in the population of the triplet state (Table 1). Triplet (ΦT) quantum yields were assessed using comparative methods reported in literature [67,68] using ZnPc in DMSO with
Φ T
s t d
=
0.65 in DMSO [68].

The triplet state quantum yields and lifetimes for all complexes are shown in Table 1. Fig. 13 A shows the triplet decay curve for complex 1 alone, 1-GQDs@GSH, and 1@GQDs-AuNPs in DMSO. The triplet decay curves obeyed second order kinetics (Fig. 13A) which is typical of MPc complexes at high concentration (1 × 10−5 M) [69]. The presence of second kinetics has been well documented [69] for phthalocyanines at high concentration. Fig. 13B shows the triplet decay curves at different concentrations and second order fit was obtained at high concentrations, while monoexponential was obtained at low concentrations.

From the curves (Fig. 13A), it can be observed that the triplet lifetime decreases from 1, 1@GQDs, to 1@GQDs-AuNPs (Table 1), corresponding to the increase in triplet quantum yields. Efficient spin orbit coupling (heavy atom effect) is known to result in shortening of triplet lifetimes and increased triplet quantum yields [70,71].

Intersystem crossing lifetimes τISC were evaluated using fluorescence lifetime (τF) and triplet quantum yields (ΦT) of the compounds, using the relationship (
τ F
Φ T
). There is an observed decrease in the τisc values from 5.6 ns for complex 1 alone to 4.8 and 3.0 for 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs, respectively (Table 1). The increase in the triplet quantum yields and subsequent decrease in intersystem crossing lifetimes following assembly of AuNPs signifies enhanced intersystem crossing in the conjugates (Table 1). This is in line with observed decrease in the triplet lifetimes for the conjugates, due to the presence of Au as a heavy metal which induces a heavy metal effect as discussed above.

The singlet oxygen quantum yield (Ф Δ ) was determined by the chemical photodegradation of the singlet oxygen quencher (DPBF) in DMSO. Singlet oxygen is a product of efficient interaction of the triplet state of photosensitizer with the ground state molecular oxygen (resulting in energy transfer from the photosensitizer to the molecular oxygen). Fig. 14 and Fig. S5 (Supporting information) show the singlet curves for the 1@GQDs-AuNPs and complex 1 alone, respectively.

Singlet oxygen (ΦΔ) quantum yields were assessed using comparative methods reported in literature [67] using ZnPc in DMSO as a standard with
Φ Δ
s t d
=
0.67 [67]. DPBF was used as a singlet oxygen quencher for complex 1 and the respective conjugates. The concentration of DPBF was lowered to 3 × 10−5 mol dm−3 for all solutions, to avoid chain reactions. DPBF degradation was spectroscopically monitored at 417 nm at predetermined time interval.

The singlet oxygen quantum yields for complex 1, 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs are summarised in Table 1. The larger ΦΔ values for 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs compared to complex 1 alone correspond to the trend in triplet quantum yields (Table 1). These results demonstrates that conjugation of complex 1 to GQDs@GSH and then to AuNPs (to form 1@GQDs and 1@GQDs-AuNPs, respectively) led to enhanced intersystem crossing to the triplet state and consequently improved singlet oxygen generation ability of the photosensitizer. The energy transfer efficiency (S Δ = Ф Δ /ФT) between excited triplet state photosensitizer and the ground state molecular oxygen of the nanoconjugates were obtained for complex 1 and its conjugates (Table 1). The S Δ value of the 1@GQDs-AuNPs of 0.96 indicates an efficient quenching of the excited triplet state of the composite by molecular oxygen.

A facile method was employed for the assembly of AuNPs unto graphene quantum dots-zinc phthalocyanines conjugates. A decrease in the fluorescence quantum yields and lifetimes was observed for the conjugated phthalocyanines. Conjugation resulted in an increase in triplet quantum yield values and a decrease in the triplet lifetimes. An important feature was the high singlet oxygen quantum yield of the Pc/GQDs@GSH/AuNPs composite as high as 87%, highlighting its potential for use as a novel PDT agent (eliminating the challenge of fluorescence based dosimetry). The results further highlight that the nanocomposites herein reported, (by combining the intrinsic properties of Pcs, GQDs@GSH and AuNPs) with improved Raman scattering properties, may serve as a novel nanoplatforms with properties vital for the determination of appropriate light doses and photosensitizer concentration, which are crucial in PDT dosimetry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa, through the DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620). We would also like to thank Rhodes University, and Dr Desmond E. Goddard for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.04.011.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
